formatDefinitions.displayName: Complete with words from the box

exercises.title: A: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap in the same line.
exercises.passage: [1]My brother has a huge comic book (1) ...... (COLLECT).[/1] [2]One of his favourite (2) ...... (HERO) is Spider-Man.[/2] [3]He started buying comics during his (3) ...... (CHILD) and now he has hundreds.[/3] [4]Some of the stories are full of exciting (4) ...... (ACT) scenes, while others are more funny than dramatic.[/4] [5]He often visits comic fairs with other (5) ...... (FAN) and sometimes dresses as famous characters.[/5] [6]He says comics are great (6) ...... (ENTERTAIN) and help him relax after school.[/6] [7]Last month, he even met a well-known comic book (7) ...... (ART) who signed one of his books.[/7] [8]He's also becoming a serious comic (8) ...... (COLLECT) and spends all his pocket money on rare editions.[/8]

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: After comic book you need a noun — the thing he collects — so use collection, not the verb collect.

wrongOptions.collect: COLLECT is the verb root — the gap needs a noun (collection) after comic book.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Don't use the root verb when the sentence needs a noun — check what word class fits after the noun before the gap.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Read the words before the gap: comic book + ___ needs a noun for the set of comics.
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: COLLECT → collection: add -ion to form the noun meaning a set of collected items.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: after a noun like comic book, use a derived noun (collection), not the verb root.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Favourite heroes is plural — he has more than one favourite character — so use heroes, not the singular hero.

wrongOptions.hero: HERO is singular — one of his favourite ___ needs the plural heroes.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the gap needs singular or plural — one of his favourite ___ implies more than one.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Phrases like one of his favourite ___ usually need a plural noun.
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: HERO → heroes: add -es for the regular plural noun.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: one of + plural noun — heroes, not hero.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: During his ___ needs a time-period noun — the years when he was a child — so use childhood.

wrongOptions.child: CHILD is a noun for a person — during his ___ needs a time noun like childhood.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Don't use the person noun (child) when the sentence needs a time period (childhood).
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: During his ___ almost always needs a time noun: childhood, youth, schooldays.
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: CHILD → childhood: add -hood to form the noun for the period of being a child.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: during his + time noun — childhood, not child.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Exciting ___ scenes needs an adjective before scenes — the type of scenes in the story — so use action.

wrongOptions.act: ACT is the verb root — exciting ___ scenes needs an adjective/noun modifier like action.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Before a plural noun (scenes), use a word that describes the type — action scenes, not act scenes.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Noun + noun compounds like action scenes or horror films use the root as a modifier.
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: ACT → action: use the noun form as a modifier before another noun (action scenes).
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: exciting + modifier + scenes — action fits as a compound noun modifier.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Other ___ at comic fairs means other people who share his hobby — a plural noun — so use fans.

wrongOptions.fan: FAN is singular — other ___ implies more than one person, so use fans.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Other + plural noun — fans, not fan.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Other usually takes a plural noun when it means other people (other fans, other students).
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: FAN → fans: add -s for the regular plural.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: other + plural countable noun — fans.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: Great ___ after great needs a noun — the thing comics provide — so use entertainment.

wrongOptions.entertain: ENTERTAIN is a verb — great ___ needs a noun like entertainment.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: After great (adjective), use a noun — entertainment — not the verb entertain.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Adjective + noun: great fun, great entertainment, great exercise — never the verb form.
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: ENTERTAIN → entertainment: add -ment to form the noun.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: great + noun — entertainment, not entertain.

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: A well-known comic book ___ who signed needs a person noun — someone who draws comics — so use artist.

wrongOptions.art: ART is the subject — the gap needs a person noun (artist) who can sign a book.

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: When the sentence describes a person (who signed), use a person noun — artist — not the subject art.
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: ART → artist: add -ist to form the noun for a person who creates art.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: a comic book + person noun — artist (who signed).

gapExplanationContent.whyCorrect: A serious comic ___ who spends money on rare editions is a person who collects — so use collector, not collection.

wrongOptions.collect: COLLECT is the verb — the gap needs a person noun (collector).
wrongOptions.collection: Collection is the set of items — here you need the person who collects (collector).

gapExplanationContent.commonMistake: Gap (1) was collection (the thing); gap (8) is collector (the person) — same root, different suffix.
gapExplanationContent.usefulTip: Same root, different gaps: -tion/-ion for things (collection), -or/-er for people (collector).
gapExplanationContent.wordFormation: COLLECT → collector: add -or to form the noun for a person who collects.
gapExplanationContent.grammarFocus: Word formation: a serious comic + person noun — collector (not collection from gap 1).

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete using the verbs in the box
exercises.title: B: Complete using the verbs in the box
exercises.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in the box. You have to use one word twice.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using the correct form of the verbs in the box. You have to use one word twice.

items.sentence: Everyone was nervous, but the singer told the band to ...... on playing.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The phrasal verb *carry on* means *continue*. After *to*, use the base form: *carry on playing*.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Phrasal verb: carry on + verb-ing = continue doing something.

wrongOptions.eat: *Eat on* is not a phrasal verb here — the band should *carry on* (continue) playing.
wrongOptions.give: *Give on* does not fit — *carry on* means keep going after feeling nervous.
wrongOptions.send: *Send on* does not match — the singer tells them to *carry on* (continue) playing.
wrongOptions.take: *Take on* means accept a challenge, not continue an activity already in progress.
wrongOptions.turn: *Turn on* means switch on — the sentence needs *carry on* (continue).

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse *carry on* (continue) with *carry* alone (hold and move something).
explanationContent.usefulTip: One bank word is used twice in this exercise — look for *carry on* in two different tenses.

items.sentence: Can you ...... the radio down a bit? I'm trying to study.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *Turn down* means reduce the volume. The pattern is *turn + object + down*.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Phrasal verb: turn down = make quieter (opposite of turn up).

wrongOptions.carry: *Carry down* does not describe volume — you *turn* a radio *down*.
wrongOptions.eat: *Eat down* is not a phrasal verb — the radio volume needs *turning down*.
wrongOptions.give: *Give down* does not fit — reduce volume with *turn down*.
wrongOptions.send: *Send down* does not match — you *turn the radio down*.
wrongOptions.take: *Take down* means remove or dismantle, not lower the volume.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse *turn down* (lower volume) with *turn up* (increase volume) later in the exercise.
explanationContent.usefulTip: *Turn* appears twice — once with *down* and once with *up*. Read the whole sentence to pick the right particle.

items.sentence: The referee ...... the player off after the dangerous tackle.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *Send off* means dismiss a player from the pitch. Past tense: *send → sent* (irregular).
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Phrasal verb + past tense: the referee *sent* the player *off* after the tackle.

wrongOptions.carry: *Carry off* means remove something — a referee *sends a player off*.
wrongOptions.eat: *Eat off* is not a phrasal verb — the referee *sent him off* the field.
wrongOptions.give: *Give off* means produce (smell/light) — not dismiss from a match.
wrongOptions.send: The bank word is *send*, but the sentence is past tense — use *sent*, not *send*.
wrongOptions.take: *Take off* means leave quickly or remove clothing — not a red-card dismissal.
wrongOptions.turn: *Turn off* means switch off — the referee *sent the player off*.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The bank gives *send*, but the gap needs the past form *sent* because the story is in the past.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Check the tense of the sentence — several answers need a past form of the bank verb.

items.sentence: We usually ...... out on Saturdays because nobody wants to cook.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *Eat out* means have a meal at a restaurant instead of at home.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Phrasal verb: eat out = dine away from home (no change to the base form here).

wrongOptions.carry: *Carry out* means perform or deliver — not go to a restaurant.
wrongOptions.give: *Give out* means distribute — not have dinner on Saturdays.
wrongOptions.send: *Send out* means dispatch — the family *eats out* on Saturdays.
wrongOptions.take: *Take out* can mean buy takeaway food, but *eat out* is the usual collocation here.
wrongOptions.turn: *Turn out* means prove to be or switch off — not dine at a restaurant.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not pick a verb only because it fits grammatically — *eat out* is a fixed phrasal verb for dining away from home.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read what follows the verb: *out on Saturdays* signals the phrasal verb *eat out*.

items.sentence: That's my favourite song! ...... it up!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *Turn it up* means increase the volume — the opposite of *turn down* in sentence 2.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Phrasal verb: turn up = make louder. Imperative: *Turn it up!*

wrongOptions.carry: *Carry up* is not used for volume — you *turn the music up*.
wrongOptions.eat: *Eat up* means finish your food — not raise the volume.
wrongOptions.give: *Give up* means stop trying — here you want louder music.
wrongOptions.send: *Send up* can mean parody something — not increase volume.
wrongOptions.take: *Take up* means start a hobby — not make music louder.

explanationContent.commonMistake: *Turn* is used twice: *turn down* (quieter) and *turn up* (louder). Match the particle to the meaning.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Exclamation marks often signal an imperative — *Turn it up!* is a command to increase volume.

items.sentence: Sophie ...... up gymnastics when she started university.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *Give up* means stop doing an activity. Past tense: *give → gave* (irregular).
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Phrasal verb + past tense: Sophie *gave up* gymnastics when she started university.

wrongOptions.carry: *Carry up* does not mean quit — Sophie *gave up* gymnastics.
wrongOptions.eat: *Eat up* means finish food — she stopped the sport, not a meal.
wrongOptions.give: The bank word is *give*, but the sentence is past tense — use *gave*, not *give*.
wrongOptions.send: *Send up* does not mean abandon a sport.
wrongOptions.take: *Take up* means start something new — Sophie did the opposite and quit.
wrongOptions.turn: *Turn up* does not fit — she *gave up* (stopped) gymnastics.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The bank shows *give*, but the past form *gave* is required after *Sophie* in a finished past action.
explanationContent.usefulTip: After a subject in the past (*Sophie … when she started*), check whether the bank verb needs a past form.

items.sentence: After a short break, the match ...... on until sunset.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *Carry on* appears again — this time in the past: *carry → carried*. The match continued until sunset.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Same phrasal verb as sentence 1, but past tense: *The match carried on* until sunset.

wrongOptions.carry: The bank word is *carry*, but the sentence is past tense — use *carried*, not *carry*.
wrongOptions.eat: *Eat on* is not a phrasal verb — the match *carried on* (continued).
wrongOptions.give: *Give on* does not fit — the match kept going: *carried on*.
wrongOptions.send: *Send on* does not mean continue — use *carried on*.
wrongOptions.take: *Take on* means accept a challenge — the match simply continued.
wrongOptions.turn: *Turn on* means switch on — the match *carried on* until sunset.

explanationContent.commonMistake: *Carry* is the word used twice — once as *carry on* (present) and once as *carried on* (past). Match the tense.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The instruction says one word is used twice — *carry* becomes *carry on* and later *carried on*.

items.sentence: Why don't you ...... up tennis? It's great exercise.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: *Take up* means start a new hobby or sport. After *Why don't you*, use the base form: *take up tennis*.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Phrasal verb: take up = begin a new activity (tennis, a language, etc.).

wrongOptions.carry: *Carry up* does not mean start a sport — you *take up tennis*.
wrongOptions.eat: *Eat up* means finish food — not begin a new activity.
wrongOptions.give: *Give up* means quit — the suggestion is to *start* tennis.
wrongOptions.send: *Send up* does not mean begin a hobby.
wrongOptions.turn: *Turn up* means arrive or increase volume — not start playing a sport.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not confuse *take up* (start) with *give up* (stop) — they are opposites.
explanationContent.usefulTip: *Why don't you …?* suggestions use the base verb: *take up*, not *took up*.

exercises.title: C: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
Emily really loves rock music. **keen**
Emily ...... rock music.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is loves, so the correct transformation is "is keen on" — keen on means the same as love or like very much.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Keen on is a fixed adjective + preposition pattern (like interested in or good at) — the preposition on is part of the meaning.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "keen" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.keen: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "keen" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Keen needs the preposition on — *keen rock music* is not correct English.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: She's really keen on photography.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "keen" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.sentence:
My dad sailed around the world when he was younger. **sailor**
My dad ...... when he was younger.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is sailed around the world, so the correct transformation is "was a sailor" — it describes his job when he was younger.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Use a noun phrase (was a sailor) to paraphrase the action verb sailed — keep the past time reference from when he was younger.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "sailor" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.sailor: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "sailor" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The keyword is a noun, so build a noun phrase (was a sailor) — do not try to repeat sailed.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: When she was young, she was a professional dancer.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "sailor" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.sentence:
Do you want to play a board game? **feel**
Do you ...... a board game?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is want to play, so the correct transformation is "feel like playing" — feel like + -ing means the same as want to.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Feel like is followed by -ing, not an infinitive — say feel like playing, not feel like to play.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "feel" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.feel: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "feel" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: After feel like, use the -ing form of the verb (playing), not the infinitive (to play).
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: Do you feel like going out tonight?
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "feel" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

items.sentence:
Harry participated in the school concert last month. **part**
Harry ...... in the school concert last month.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is participated in, so the correct transformation is "took part" — took part in has the same meaning.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Participate in and take part in are parallel expressions — the preposition in stays in the second sentence.
explanationContent.wordCountMistake: Write between 2 and 5 words, including the keyword "part" unchanged. Contractions such as don't count as one word.

wrongOptions.part: Do not leave out or alter the keyword "part" — it must appear in your answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Use the past form took part to match last month — present take part would be wrong.
explanationContent.similarExample: Similar: She took part in a school play last year.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Count your words (2–5) before you check — include "part" unchanged and read the completed second sentence aloud.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: "plays" is the only option that fits the grammar and meaning here.

wrongOptions.A: "play" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "plays" is correct.
wrongOptions.play: "play" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "plays" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "is playing" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "plays" is correct.
wrongOptions.is playing: "is playing" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "plays" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "played" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "plays" is correct.
wrongOptions.played: "played" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "plays" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: "went" is the only option that fits the grammar and meaning here.

wrongOptions.go: "go" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "went" is correct.

items.sentenceAfter: TV when the lights went out.

wrongOptions.A: "watched" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "was watching" is correct.
wrongOptions.watched: "watched" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "was watching" is correct.

items.sentenceAfter: play chess every day, but now I don't have time.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: "used to" is the only option that fits the grammar and meaning here.

wrongOptions.B: "use to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "used to" is correct.
wrongOptions.use to: "use to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "used to" is correct.
wrongOptions.C: "am used to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "used to" is correct.
wrongOptions.am used to: "am used to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "used to" is correct.
wrongOptions.D: "was used to" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "used to" is correct.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: E: Match the two halves of the sentences
exercises.title: E: Match the two halves of the sentences
exercises.instructions: Match the two halves of the sentences.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap a numbered beginning, then tap the matching ending letter.

items.sentence: I used to play the guitar every evening **D** when I was at secondary school.
items.answer: D – when I was at secondary school.
acceptedAnswers: D – when I was at secondary school.

items.sentence: While we were watching the concert **B** somebody's phone started ringing.
My brother loves board games and plays them **F** with his friends every Friday.
items.answer: F – with his friends every Friday.
acceptedAnswers: F – with his friends every Friday.

items.sentence: She doesn't usually go to the gym **A** because she prefers running outside.
items.answer: A – because she prefers running outside.
acceptedAnswers: A – because she prefers running outside.

items.sentence: They were training for the final match **C** until late every evening.
I think classical music is really relaxing **E** and I listen to it while I study.
items.answer: E – and I listen to it while I study.
acceptedAnswers: E – and I listen to it while I study.
